mission.title: Install and Configure Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)
mission.description: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and scaling of containerized applications using Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking for a robust, scalable, and fully managed Kubernetes solution. Key trade-offs include reliance on Google Cloud infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the Google Cloud SDK
steps.description:
The Google Cloud SDK provides the necessary command-line tools to interact with GKE.

```bash

curl https://sdk.cloud.google.com | bash

exec -l $SHELL

gcloud init

```

> This installs the Google Cloud SDK.
> Reload the shell to update the environment.
> Initialize the SDK and set up your Google Cloud project.

steps.title: Create a GKE Cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a GKE cluster with the specified Kubernetes version and node count.

```bash

gcloud container clusters create my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a --num-nodes 3 --cluster-version 1.31

```

> Change 'my-gke-cluster' to your desired cluster name.
commands: gcloud container clusters create my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a --num-nodes 3 --cluster-version 1.31

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the newly created GKE cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a

```

> Replace 'my-gke-cluster' with your cluster name.
commands: gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your GKE cluster.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up autoscaling and monitoring for your GKE cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes 1 --max-nodes 5 --zone us-central1-a

gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-cloud-logging --enable-cloud-monitoring --zone us-central1-a

```

> Enables cluster autoscaling.
> Enables logging and monitoring.
commands: gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes 1 --max-nodes 5 --zone us-central1-a
gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-cloud-logging --enable-cloud-monitoring --zone us-central1-a

uninstall.title: Delete the GKE Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the GKE cluster and all associated resources, which may lead to data loss.

```bash

gcloud container clusters delete my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a --quiet

```

> Replace 'my-gke-cluster' with your cluster name.
commands: gcloud container clusters delete my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a --quiet

uninstall.title: Clean Up Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
This command ensures that any orphaned resources such as load balancers and persistent disks are deleted.

```bash

gcloud compute addresses delete my-gke-cluster --region us-central1

```

> Replace with your specific resources if needed.
commands: gcloud compute addresses delete my-gke-cluster --region us-central1

upgrade.title: Upgrade the GKE Cluster
upgrade.description:
Backup your workloads, upgrade the cluster, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json > backup.json

gcloud container clusters upgrade my-gke-cluster --cluster-version 1.31 --zone us-central1-a

kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup current workloads.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that nodes are upgraded.
commands: gcloud container clusters upgrade my-gke-cluster --cluster-version 1.31 --zone us-central1-a

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the cluster is not running or kubectl is not configured correctly.

**Fix:**
Run 'gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a' to reconfigure kubectl.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule new pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the number of nodes or adjust resource requests for your pods.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unauthorized' when accessing the cluster
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if your IAM permissions are insufficient.

**Fix:**
Ensure your user has the 'Kubernetes Engine Admin' role.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in 'Pending' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates resource constraints or misconfigured requests.

**Fix:**
Check resource requests and limits, and ensure sufficient resources are available.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for PodSecurity admission, improved workload identity features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, review the new PodSecurity admission policies.

resolution.summary: You should see the nodes listed with a 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.
codeSnippets: gcloud container clusters create my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a --num-nodes 3 --cluster-version 1.31
gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone us-central1-a
gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes 1 --max-nodes 5 --zone us-central1-a
gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-cloud-logging --enable-cloud-monitoring --zone us-central1-a

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Google Cloud SDK installed and configured.
